title: Press freedom rank
detail: India ranked 140 in RSF's 2014 index, 159 in 2024, 151 in 2025, and 157 in 2026. RSF scores fell from 59.66 (2014) to 31.96 (2026), but methodology changes mean ranks and scores should be read carefully rather than as a single unbroken series.

title: Crimes against women
detail: NCRB-linked figures show total registered crimes against women rose from 337,922 in 2014 to 448,211 in 2023.

title: Reported crime rate against women
detail: The NCRB-linked rate of registered crimes against women rose from 56.3 per lakh female population in 2014 to 66.2 in 2023. This population-adjusted series is still based on police registration; it does not measure the true prevalence of violence or prove that every change reflects a change in underlying incidence.

title: Total cognizable crime registered
detail: NCRB figures reported by the Ministry of Home Affairs recorded 37,63,102 IPC cases and 24,78,467 Special and Local Laws cases in 2023, for 62,41,569 total cognizable cases. This is a national registration snapshot, not a measure of all crime occurring or of a single government's causal responsibility.

title: Registered murder cases
detail: NCRB figures show registered murder cases falling from 33,981 in 2014 to 27,721 in 2023. The 2023 cases resulted in 28,587 recorded murder deaths. This is a police-registration series; it excludes killings not recorded or classified as murder and does not explain the causes of the change.

title: Registered cybercrime cases
detail: NCRB figures reported to Parliament show registered cybercrime cases rising from 9,622 in 2014 to 86,420 in 2023. The series reflects police registration and changing awareness, law, reporting, and classification; it is not a direct estimate of all cybercrime or a standalone measure of government performance.

title: Registered riot cases
detail: NCRB figures show registered riot cases falling from 66,042 in 2014 to 39,260 in 2023. This category is based on police classification under IPC provisions; it does not capture every episode of political or communal violence and does not by itself establish why the number changed.

title: POCSO 2014 justice pipeline
detail: The Ministry of Women and Child Development's NCRB 2014 table recorded 8,904 POCSO cases, 6,573 cases charge-sheeted, 406 trials completed, and 100 convictions, producing a 24.6% conviction rate among completed trials. This is a 2014 disposal snapshot, not a conviction rate for all cases registered that year or a measure of current performance.

title: Registered crimes against Scheduled Castes
detail: NCRB-linked official figures show registered crimes against Scheduled Castes rising from 47,064 cases in 2014 to 57,789 in 2023. This is a recorded-case series under the relevant crime categories; it cannot measure unreported caste violence or establish that every change reflects a change in underlying incidence.

title: Registered crimes against Scheduled Tribes
detail: NCRB figures show total registered crimes against Scheduled Tribes rising from 11,451 cases in 2014 to 12,960 in 2023. The series includes non-atrocity cases as well as cases recorded under relevant atrocity categories; it measures reported registration, not the full prevalence of violence or discrimination.

title: IPC conviction rate
detail: NCRB's national IPC conviction rate was 45.1% in 2014 and 54.0% in 2023. Conviction rate is calculated from cases in which trials were completed during the relevant disposal period; it is not the percentage of that year's registered cases that were solved, and it does not show how long the cases had been pending.

title: Rape conviction rate
detail: NCRB tables report a rape conviction rate of 28.0% in 2014 and 22.7% in 2023. The rate is calculated from rape cases in which trials were completed during the disposal period, not from all cases registered in that year; it therefore does not mean that 77.3% of 2023 reports were acquitted.

title: IPC chargesheeting rate
detail: NCRB-based national figures show the IPC chargesheeting rate falling from 79.6% in 2014 to 72.7% in 2023. Chargesheeting rate concerns police investigation outcomes for cases taken up, not conviction, acquittal, or the percentage of all reported crimes solved.

title: Registered human-trafficking cases
detail: NCRB-linked figures report 5,466 registered human-trafficking cases in 2014 and 2,183 in 2023. This is a police-registration series and the legal reporting framework and crime-head definitions can change over time; it does not estimate the true number of people trafficked or rescued.

title: Registered child kidnapping and abduction cases
detail: NCRB-linked figures show registered kidnapping and abduction of children rising from 37,854 cases in 2014 to 79,884 in 2023. This is a police-registration series; kidnapping, missing-person reports, recovery totals, and trafficking are separate measures and should not be merged.

title: Reported rape cases
detail: Official NCRB reported rape cases fell from 36,735 in 2014 to 29,670 in 2023. Year-to-year comparisons remain sensitive to reporting rates and classification changes.

title: Lawmakers and crimes against women
detail: ADR's 2024 affidavit analysis found 151 sitting MPs/MLAs had declared cases related to crimes against women. BJP had the highest party count at 54.

title: Rupee pressure
detail: Reuters-linked reports showed the rupee breaching 96 per US dollar in May 2026. The driver mix includes oil shocks, yields, capital flows, and domestic macro pressure.

title: GDP per capita
detail: World Bank current-dollar GDP per capita rose from $1,553.9 in 2014 to $2,702.5 in 2025. This is a real income-level improvement on that series, though current-dollar values move with exchange rates and inflation and do not by themselves measure distributional fairness or democratic quality.

title: CPI inflation
detail: World Bank annual CPI inflation was lower in 2025 than in 2014. This is an improvement on that metric, even though food and local price stress can diverge from annual averages.

title: Internet users
detail: World Bank data shows individuals using the internet rising from 16.5% in 2016 to 70.0% in 2025. Higher connectivity coexists with rising shutdown counts; the two series measure different things and should not be collapsed into a single 'digital freedom' claim.

title: Unemployment rate
detail: PLFS usual-status unemployment fell from 6.0% in 2017-18 to 3.1% in 2025 on the cited PIB releases. Treat the comparison carefully: PLFS sampling and definitions differ from older employment measures, and a lower unemployment rate is not the same as secure formal work.

title: Lok Sabha turnout
detail: Election Commission atlas figures show turnout broadly flat between the 2014 and 2024 Lok Sabha elections, with the 2024 note excluding the uncontested Surat seat.

title: Lok Sabha electorate
detail: The electorate rose by about 14.57 crore from 2014 to 2024, while votes polled rose from 55.42 crore to 64.64 crore.

title: Winning MPs with criminal cases
detail: ADR affidavit analysis shows winning Lok Sabha candidates with declared criminal cases rose from 185 of 542 in 2014 to 251 of 543 in 2024.

title: Winning MPs with serious criminal cases
detail: ADR/NEW figures show winning Lok Sabha candidates with declared serious criminal cases rose from 112 of 542 in 2014 to 170 of 543 in 2024.

title: Crorepati winning MPs
detail: ADR/NEW figures show the share of winning Lok Sabha candidates declaring assets above Rs 1 crore rose from 82% in 2014 to 93% in 2024.

title: Freedom House status
detail: Freedom House assessed India as Free in Freedom in the World 2014 (Political Rights 2, Civil Liberties 3 on the old 1–7 scale) and Partly Free with a 62/100 aggregate score in 2026. The rating systems are not directly numeric-comparable; the status change is the comparable claim.

title: Internet shutdowns
detail: SFLC's India shutdown tracker lists 6 shutdowns in 2014 and 54 in 2025, with 2026 still incomplete at 21 YTD in the captured research note. HRW separately documented the human-cost pattern, including Kashmir's 213-day internet blackout after August 2019.

title: Suicide incidence
detail: NCRB-linked data shows total reported suicides rose from 131,666 in 2014 to 171,418 in 2023. These numbers depend on state and UT reporting.

title: World Happiness Report rank
detail: India ranked 126th of 143 countries in the 2024 World Happiness Report, 118th of 147 in 2025, and 116th of 147 in 2026. The rank improved across these releases, but country counts, report methodology, and the survey period mean this is not a direct measure of government-caused happiness change.

title: Reported suicide rate
detail: NCRB's reported suicide rate was 10.6 per 100,000 people in 2014 and 12.3 per 100,000 in 2023. This is a civil-registration and police-recorded series, not a modelled estimate of every suicide; changes can reflect reporting and population differences.

title: Student suicide deaths
detail: NCRB figures cited in parliamentary and official reporting show student suicide deaths rising from 8,068 in 2014 to 13,892 in 2023. The Ministry of Education also reported 13,044 student suicide deaths in 2022, equal to 7.6% of all recorded suicide deaths that year. The series records deaths classified by NCRB under student status; it does not establish a single cause, and it should not be reduced to an exam-pressure statistic.

title: Registered crimes against children
detail: NCRB figures report registered crimes against children rising from 89,423 cases in 2014 to 177,335 in 2023. A parliamentary answer also recorded 128,531 cases in 2020 and 149,404 in 2021, a 16.2% increase. This is a broad registered-case category covering multiple offences, not a rape-only series; changes can reflect reporting, registration, legal classification, and population differences.

title: Cow-vigilante killings (HRW tally)
detail: HRW counted at least 44 people killed in cow-protection vigilante attacks from May 2015 to December 2018, including 36 Muslims, with about 280 injured across more than 100 incidents. This is a rights-group tally for a defined window, not an official NCRB series.

title: Kashmir internet blackout days
detail: HRW reported that the Jammu and Kashmir internet shutdown beginning August 2019 lasted 213 days until March 2020, while mobile 4G access remained restricted for 550 days. These are duration measures for one extreme episode, not annual nationwide shutdown counts.

title: Modi tenure tracked
detail: Calculated from Narendra Modi's oath as Prime Minister on 26 May 2014 through 19 July 2026. This is a date calculation, not an official duration label; it updates when the reference date changes.

title: BJP-led governments at the Centre
detail: A calendar calculation combining Atal Bihari Vajpayee's BJP-led Union governments in 1996 and 1998-2004 with Narendra Modi's Union tenure from 26 May 2014 through 19 July 2026. The figure is presented as a transparent calculation, not a constitutional or ECI statistic.

title: Time remaining in current Lok Sabha term
detail: Calendar countdown from 19 July 2026 to the scheduled five-year endpoint used for the Eighteenth Lok Sabha. Parliament can be dissolved earlier, so this is a remaining-term estimate rather than a guarantee.

title: Consequential railway accidents
detail: A Ministry of Railways release reported consequential train accidents falling from 135 in 2014-15 to 40 in 2023-24. The same release reported the accident rate per million train-kilometres falling from 0.11 to 0.03. These are official railway definitions and should not be conflated with every rail incident or every passenger death.

title: Extreme poverty rate
detail: The World Bank's June 2025 update estimated India's extreme-poverty rate at 16.2% in 2011-12 and 2.3% in 2022-23 using its revised $2.15-a-day line and MMRP-based welfare aggregate. This series begins before 2014 and reflects survey, price, and methodology revisions; it should not be presented as a simple annual government-performance score.

title: Female labour-force participation
detail: Official PLFS usual-status estimates for women aged 15 years and above rose from 23.3% in 2017-18 to 41.7% in 2023-24. The survey periods and definitions matter, and participation is not the same as formal employment, earnings, or job quality.

title: Female Lok Sabha turnout
detail: Election Commission figures show female turnout at 65.63% in the 2014 Lok Sabha election and 65.78% in 2024. In 2024, female turnout was 0.23 percentage points above male turnout; the 2024 all-India comparison excludes the uncontested Surat constituency where noted by the ECI.

title: Electoral-bond receipts
detail: Election Commission disclosures and reporting based on the released bond data show the BJP encashed about Rs 6,060 crore in electoral bonds between April 2019 and January 2024, the largest party total in the published dataset. The Supreme Court struck the scheme down in February 2024; this figure is a historical receipt total, not a current funding stream.

title: NEET exam-integrity scale
detail: The 2024 NEET-UG controversy affected more than 23 lakh candidates through uncertainty over the result and alleged paper-leak and grace-mark irregularities. In the separate 2026 Latur investigation, the CBI told a Delhi court that 111 of 136 recovered questions matched NTA master sets. These are two different years and measures, shown together only to indicate scale and not as one combined rate.

title: Road-accident fatalities
detail: Ministry of Road Transport and Highways data reports road-accident deaths rising from 139,671 in 2014 to 177,175 in 2024. The ministry's series is based on information supplied by State and Union Territory police departments; it measures reported fatalities, not an independently audited count of every crash.

title: UPI monthly transaction volume
detail: NPCI and PIB report UPI monthly transaction volume rising from about 0.01 crore in 2016 to 1,673 crore in December 2024. This measures payment-system usage and access, not income, consumption quality, or the distribution of digital access across households.

title: Installed renewable-energy capacity
detail: MNRE/PIB figures report installed renewable-energy capacity rising from 76.38 GW in March 2014 to 274.68 GW in March 2026. This is installed capacity, not electricity actually generated, emissions avoided, storage availability, or household reliability.

title: BJP Lok Sabha seats
detail: Election Commission results show the BJP winning 282 Lok Sabha seats in 2014, 303 in 2019, and 240 in 2024. The 2024 result left the BJP short of a single-party majority and required the NDA coalition to form the government; seat count is not the same as vote share or policy performance.

title: BJP Lok Sabha vote share
detail: Election Commission data reports the BJP's share of valid votes at 31.34% in 2014, 37.70% in 2019, and 36.56% in 2024. The 2024 fall in seats was much larger than the change in national vote share, illustrating how first-past-the-post seat outcomes depend on geographic distribution and alliances.

title: Ujjwala LPG connections
detail: PMUY was launched in May 2016 and the government reported 10.33 crore connections released by 1 July 2024. The connection count records access to an LPG connection; it does not by itself establish regular refill affordability or exclusive use of LPG for cooking.

title: Rural household tap-water connections
detail: Jal Jeevan Mission reporting shows rural households with tap-water connections rising from 3.23 crore (16.7%) at the August 2019 launch to 15.82 crore (about 82%) by March 2026. The government defines the service target as 55 litres per capita per day of prescribed-quality water, but reported connection coverage is not the same as independently verified regular supply or water quality.

title: Rural sanitation coverage
detail: Government reporting says rural sanitation coverage rose from 39% in 2014 to 100% in 2019, with more than 10 crore household latrines constructed under SBM(G). An independent 2022 Swachh Survekshan Grameen finding reported 95.4% of rural households had access to toilets, so declared ODF status and measured household access are shown as different evidence points.

title: PMAY-Gramin completed houses
detail: The Rural Development Ministry reported 2.81 crore PMAY-Gramin houses completed as of 24 July 2025 against 3.84 crore sanctioned beneficiaries. The programme began in 2016; the zero baseline here means no PMAY-G houses existed before this scheme, not that India had no rural housing construction before 2016.

title: National LPG coverage
detail: A government release reported national LPG coverage at 55.9% on 1 April 2014 and described coverage as near saturation by 1 April 2023. This is a reported access-coverage series; it does not establish that every household could afford regular refills or used LPG as its primary cooking fuel.

title: Direct Benefit Transfer scale
detail: The Finance Ministry reported PFMS-linked DBT transactions rising from 2.19 crore in FY2014-15 to 181.64 crore in FY2024-25 through 30 November 2024. The same release reported Rs 20.23 lakh crore paid through this PFMS-linked series from 2014. The endpoint is a partial financial year and the series covers PFMS-linked DBT, not every welfare payment in India.

title: Jan Dhan account access
detail: The Finance Ministry reported PMJDY accounts rising from 14.72 crore in 2015 to 58.16 crore as of 29 April 2026, with deposits of Rs 3.02 lakh crore at the latter date. Account ownership and deposits measure formal financial access; they do not by themselves show income security, account activity, or whether every intended beneficiary received a benefit.

title: Undertrial prison population
detail: A Ministry of Home Affairs answer using NCRB Prison Statistics reported undertrial prisoners rising from 282,879 at 31 December 2014 to 427,165 at 31 December 2021. Prison administration is primarily a State subject, and the series ends in 2021 in this cited parliamentary answer; it should not be read as a direct attribution of every change to the Union government.

title: Prison occupancy
detail: A December 2025 Ministry of Home Affairs answer citing NCRB Prison Statistics reported 530,333 prisoners against available capacity of 439,119 on 31 December 2023, equivalent to about 120.8% occupancy. This is a national snapshot, and prison administration is primarily a State subject; the number describes capacity pressure rather than assigning cause to one government.

title: Reported police-custody deaths
detail: A Ministry of Home Affairs parliamentary answer reported 145 police-custody deaths in 2016-17, 146 in 2017-18, and 136 in 2018-19. These are reported cases in the answer's financial-year series, not findings that every death was caused by police misconduct; police and public order are primarily State subjects.

title: Supreme Court and High Court pendency
detail: The Department of Justice reported 92,876 cases pending in the Supreme Court and 63,98,686 in the High Courts as of 16 March 2026, a combined 64,91,562 cases. This is a dated snapshot from the National Judicial Data Grid, not a complete count of all trial-court cases and not a claim that all pendency has one cause.

title: District and subordinate-court pendency
detail: The Department of Justice reported 49,956,834 cases pending in District and Subordinate Courts as of 13 March 2026; 14,806,291 had been pending for more than five years. This NJDG snapshot covers the lower-court system and is separate from the Supreme Court and High Court total shown above.

title: Judicial-officer vacancies
detail: The Department of Justice reports sanctioned and working strength in District and Subordinate Courts rising from 19,518 and 15,115 on 1 January 2014 to 24,514 and 19,341 on 27 January 2022. The implied vacancy count rose from 4,403 to 5,173. Recruitment is primarily the responsibility of State Governments and High Courts, so this is an institutional-capacity series rather than a Union-only performance measure.

title: High Court judge vacancies
detail: The Department of Justice reported 1,122 sanctioned High Court judge posts, 789 working judges, and 333 vacancies as of 1 June 2026. The table includes permanent and additional-judge strength; it is a dated staffing snapshot and does not by itself explain appointment timing or court workload.

title: Women elected to Lok Sabha
detail: Election Commission data records 62 women elected in 2014, 78 in 2019, and 74 in 2024. Women's representation peaked in 2019 at 14.4% of the House and was 13.6% in 2024. This measures elected representation, not the share of women candidates, cabinet representation, or the effect of the 2023 reservation law, whose implementation depends on later delimitation and census steps.

title: Out-of-pocket health spending
detail: National Health Accounts estimates report household out-of-pocket expenditure falling from 64.2% of Total Health Expenditure in 2013-14 to 39.4% in 2021-22. The same release reports Government Health Expenditure's share rising from 28.6% to 48.0% across the period. These are national accounting estimates, not a guarantee that every household experienced lower medical costs.

title: Registered crimes against senior citizens
detail: A parliamentary answer citing NCRB recorded 18,714 registered crimes against people above 60 in 2014 and 20,532 in 2015. NCRB said this dedicated senior-citizen series began in January 2014, so earlier comparisons are not like-for-like; these figures measure police registration, not the full prevalence of abuse or neglect.

title: Deaths in prisons
detail: NCRB prison statistics recorded 1,701 inmate deaths in 2014, including 101 classified as unnatural, and 1,972 deaths in 2023, including 150 classified as unnatural. The 2023 report recorded 96 suicides. These classifications are supplied by States/UTs; the NCRB notes that the causes of 35 Rajasthan deaths were unknown in 2023.

title: Undertrial share of prison population
detail: NCRB prison statistics show undertrial prisoners made up about 67.6% of India's prison population at the end of 2014 and 73.5% at the end of 2023. Undertrial status means a person has not been convicted; this is a detention and judicial-pendency measure, not a crime or guilt measure.

title: Prison occupancy rate
detail: NCRB Prison Statistics recorded an all-India occupancy rate of 117.4% at the end of 2014 and 120.8% at the end of 2023. A rate above 100% means the inmate population exceeded sanctioned capacity; this is a capacity-pressure measure, not a direct measure of prison conditions in every facility.

title: Prison staff vacancy
detail: NCRB Prison Statistics India 2023 recorded 94,458 sanctioned jail-staff posts and 63,490 staff actually in position, leaving 30,968 vacancies, or about 32.8% of sanctioned strength. The same year had 530,333 inmates, equivalent to roughly 8.4 inmates per actual staff member. This is an all-India snapshot and staffing categories vary by State/UT.

title: Women in state police forces
detail: BPR&D data cited by the Union government shows women were 6.11% of the actual State/UT police strength on 1 January 2014 and 11.75% on 1 January 2022. The series measures representation, not whether police services are accessible or safe for women; the Union government has repeatedly advised States to target 33% representation.

title: State/UT police vacancy
detail: Official BPR&D/MHA data reported 2,283,646 sanctioned State/UT police posts and 1,722,786 personnel in position on 1 January 2014, leaving 560,860 vacancies (about 24.5%). On 1 January 2020, the comparable State/UT categories had 2,623,225 sanctioned and 2,091,488 actual personnel, leaving 531,737 vacancies (about 20.3%). The later vacancy count was slightly lower while the sanctioned establishment and actual force were larger. Police and public order are State subjects, so this is a national capacity comparison rather than a measure attributable to the Union government alone.

title: IAS and IPS cadre vacancies
detail: A PIB reply reported that, as of 1 January 2020, the authorized IAS strength was 6,715 with 5,205 officers in position, leaving 1,510 vacancies (about 22.5%). The authorized IPS strength was 4,982 with 4,074 in position, leaving 908 vacancies (about 18.2%). These are All India Services cadre vacancies, distinct from the much larger State/UT police-force vacancy series.

title: Police population capacity
detail: BPR&D's Data on Police Organizations reported a sanctioned police-population ratio of 195.39 personnel per lakh population as of 1 January 2020. Using the same release's sanctioned and actual State/UT force totals, the implied actual ratio is about 155.9 per lakh. The second figure is calculated from the official totals and should be read as an estimate, not as a separately published ratio.

title: Police operational infrastructure
detail: BPR&D's 1 January 2020 State/UT police snapshot listed 800 police districts, 16,955 sanctioned police stations, 318 State Armed Police battalions, 202,925 police vehicles, and 460,220 CCTV cameras. These are infrastructure and equipment counts, not measures of whether stations were adequately staffed, maintained, accessible, or effective in individual cases.

title: Police recruitment and training
value: 119,069 personnel recruited; Rs 1,566.85 crore training spend
detail: BPR&D's State/UT police organization data reported 119,069 police personnel recruited during 2019 and police-training expenditure of Rs 1,566.85 crore in financial year 2019-20. The release does not establish whether recruitment matched attrition, whether spending was evenly distributed, or whether training improved case outcomes.

title: Crime against women justice pipeline
detail: A parliamentary answer reproducing NCRB tables reported 405,326 total crimes against women registered in 2019, with 295,579 cases charge-sheeted, 30,968 cases convicted, and 1,582,313 cases pending trial at year-end. For 2020 it reported 371,503 registered, 291,303 charge-sheeted, 21,551 convicted, and 1,789,601 pending trial. These are different pipeline stages and cannot be read as a same-year conviction rate; police and prosecution responsibilities are primarily with States/UTs.

title: Murder with rape or gang rape cases
detail: NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 284 registered cases under the crime head 'murder with rape/gang rape' in 2019 and 219 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 44 in 2019 and 17 in 2020, while cases pending trial at year-end were 776 and 907 respectively. These are official crime-head and court-disposal figures; they do not establish that every registered case occurred in the same year or that the conviction count corresponds only to that year's registrations.

title: Dowry death justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced by Parliament recorded 7,141 dowry-death cases registered in 2019 and 6,966 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 1,280 and 956 respectively, while cases pending trial at year-end were 46,507 and 50,513. As with other NCRB pipeline figures, conviction counts relate to trials completed during the disposal period and cannot be divided directly by that year's registrations.

title: POCSO justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables in a parliamentary record report 47,324 POCSO cases registered in 2019, 47,221 in 2020, and 53,874 in 2021. A separate NCRB justice-pipeline table reported 45,989 and 46,123 registrations for 2019 and 2020 alongside 5,549 and 3,648 convictions and 131,931 and 166,055 cases pending trial. The source tables use different reporting cuts; the registration trend is shown separately from court-disposal figures rather than mixing them into a false conviction rate.

title: Abetment to suicide of women justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 5,008 cases under the crime head 'abetment to suicide of women' in 2019 and 5,040 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 399 and 171 respectively, while cases pending trial at year-end were 18,043 and 21,189. The crime-head label does not establish the reason for each death; these figures describe police registration and court-pipeline status only.
direction: Slightly higher registrations; lower convictions; higher pending stock

title: Dowry Prohibition Act justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 13,307 cases registered under the Dowry Prohibition Act in 2019 and 10,366 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 654 and 802 respectively, while cases pending trial at year-end were 48,127 and 54,484. Registration, conviction, and pending-trial figures describe different stages of the system and should not be treated as a same-year conviction rate.

title: Acid-attack justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 150 cases under the acid-attack crime head in 2019 and 105 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 10 in each year, while cases pending trial at year-end were 428 and 503. These figures measure recorded cases and court-pipeline status; they do not capture unreported attacks, injury severity, or long-term rehabilitation outcomes.

title: Domestic violence justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 553 cases under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act in 2019 and 446 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 23 and 53 respectively, while cases pending trial at year-end were 1,970 and 2,247. The figures are a narrow registered-case and court-pipeline snapshot, not a prevalence estimate for domestic abuse.

title: Cybercrime against women justice pipeline
detail: The NCRB tables reproduced in a parliamentary answer recorded 1,635 cases under the cyber-crimes/IT Act crime head against women in 2019 and 2,334 in 2020. Convictions recorded in the disposal data were 7 and 11 respectively, while cases pending trial at year-end were 1,261 and 2,029. This is a narrow registered-case and court-pipeline category, not an estimate of all online abuse or a direct measure of digital safety.

title: Global Innovation Index
detail: WIPO's Global Innovation Index 2024 ranks India 39th of 133 economies with a score of 38.3. WIPO reports that India moved up one place from the previous edition and continued to lead its Central and Southern Asia region. This measures innovation-system inputs and outputs, not general prosperity, public-service quality, or equality.

title: Global Gender Gap Index
detail: The World Economic Forum's Global Gender Gap Report 2025 ranks India 131st of 148 economies with 64.4% of the measured gender gap closed. The index covers economic opportunity, education, health, and political leadership. It is a comparative parity index and does not measure the full incidence of violence, legal rights, unpaid work, or lived experience.

title: Global Hunger Index
detail: The 2024 Global Hunger Index country table places India at rank 105 of 127 countries with a score of 27.3, classified as serious. The index combines undernourishment, child wasting, child stunting, and child mortality. The report cautions that rankings and scores from different editions cannot be directly compared because the underlying data and country coverage change.

title: UN Human Development Index
detail: The UNDP Human Development Report 2025 statistical table places India at rank 130 of 193 countries and territories for 2023, up from rank 133 for 2022. India’s 2023 HDI value was 0.685, with life expectancy of 72.0 years, expected years of schooling of 13.0, mean years of schooling of 6.9, and GNI per capita of US$9,047 in 2021 PPP terms. Rank changes across editions should be read with the report's methodology and revised-data caveats.
direction: Higher rank; medium human development category

title: Tele-MANAS mental-health access
detail: A December 2025 Rajya Sabha answer reported that 36 States/UTs had set up 53 Tele-MANAS cells, services were available in 20 languages, and more than 2,982,000 calls had been handled by 27 November 2025. This measures service reach and usage, not unique callers, clinical outcomes, or whether demand was fully met. The same answer reported 47 government-run mental hospitals.

title: Districts sanctioned for mental-health programme
detail: A December 2025 Ministry of Home Affairs parliamentary answer stated that the District Mental Health Programme had been sanctioned for implementation in 767 districts. This is a government programme-coverage measure, not proof of equal staffing, service availability, treatment quality, or suicide prevention in every district.

title: Crime against women justice pipeline, 2021-2022
detail: A Rajya Sabha answer reproducing NCRB tables recorded 428,278 crime-against-women cases registered in 2021, 332,083 cases charge-sheeted, and 23,243 cases convicted. For 2022 it recorded 445,256 registered, 351,183 charge-sheeted, and 38,136 convicted. These are different pipeline stages and cannot be read as a same-year conviction rate; the answer also notes that police and public-order responsibilities rest primarily with States/UTs.
direction: Higher registrations; higher charge-sheeting and convictions

title: Cybercrime against women registrations
detail: A Ministry of Home Affairs parliamentary answer using NCRB data recorded 10,730 cybercrime cases against women in 2021, 14,409 in 2022, and 19,510 in 2023. This is a registered-case series and does not measure all online abuse, under-reporting, investigation quality, or convictions.

title: Fast-track courts for rape and POCSO cases
value: 733 functional courts, including 413 exclusive POCSO courts
detail: A parliamentary answer stated that the Centrally Sponsored Scheme for 1,023 Fast Track Special Courts for rape and POCSO cases had 733 functional courts, including 413 exclusive POCSO courts, across 28 States/UTs. The figure is a dated implementation snapshot from the answer; it is not a measure of case disposal speed, conviction, geographic access, or whether the full sanctioned scheme was operational everywhere.

title: Corruption Perceptions Index
detail: Transparency International's 2024 Corruption Perceptions Index places India at rank 96 of 180 with a score of 38 out of 100. The CPI measures perceived public-sector corruption using expert and business assessments; it is not a count of proven cases, convictions, individual bribes, or a direct measure of any one government's causal responsibility.

title: Rule of Law Index
detail: The World Justice Project's India profile places India 79th of 142 countries in 2024 and 86th of 143 in 2025. The overall score is shown as 0.49 in both profiles when rounded to two decimals, while the 2025 profile records a year-over-year score change of -0.01 before rounding. In 2025, India's lowest factor scores were Criminal Justice (0.39), Absence of Corruption (0.40), and Civil Justice (0.42). Because the country set and unrounded values matter, rank movement should not be read as a perfectly like-for-like decline.
direction: Lower rank; score broadly flat when rounded

title: Global Peace Index
detail: The Institute for Economics & Peace's Global Peace Index 2025 ranks India 115th of 163 countries with an overall score of 2.229. India ranked fourth in South Asia in the report. The index combines 23 indicators across ongoing conflict, safety and security, and militarisation; it is not a count of crimes, terrorist attacks, civilian deaths, or a direct attribution of outcomes to a single government.

title: EIU Democracy Index
value: Score 7.92 in 2014 to 7.04 in 2022; flawed democracy
detail: The Economist Intelligence Unit's Democracy Index 2022 report gives India a score of 7.92 in its 2014 historical series and 7.04 in 2022; India ranked 46th of 167 in 2022 and was classified as a flawed democracy. The index averages 60 indicators across electoral process and pluralism, functioning of government, political participation, political culture, and civil liberties. It is an external assessment, not a direct causal estimate of any administration's impact.

title: Extreme poverty at the international line
detail: A World Bank technical note using updated Poverty and Inequality Platform estimates reports that India's extreme-poverty rate at the $2.15 per person per day line in 2017 purchasing-power-parity dollars fell from 16.2% in 2011-12 to 2.3% in 2022-23. This is a substantial improvement on that measure, but the comparison spans different survey periods and revised PPP/methodological estimates; it does not mean poverty, deprivation, or vulnerability disappeared.

title: Maternal mortality ratio
detail: UNICEF India reports that India's national Sample Registration System maternal mortality ratio declined from 130 per 100,000 live births in 2014-16 to 97 in 2018-20. This is an improvement in the official national estimate, but it is a period-based ratio rather than a yearly count and does not describe unequal outcomes across states or communities.

title: Access to electricity
detail: World Bank data reports that 99.5% of India's population had access to electricity in 2023. This is a strong access snapshot, but the indicator does not measure affordability, hours of supply, voltage quality, household-level reliability, or regional inequality.

title: Basic sanitation access
detail: UNICEF's SDG country dashboard reports that 78% of India's population used at least basic sanitation services in 2022. The same dashboard reports 52% safely managed sanitation and 11% open defecation, showing why toilet access and safe sanitation should be tracked separately.

title: Routine child immunisation
detail: UNICEF reports that 93% of surviving infants received the third DTP-containing vaccine dose in 2022, and its latest 2025 estimates put India at 97% coverage. Coverage is a major health-access gain, but national averages can hide districts with lower uptake and do not mean every child is fully immunised.

title: Zero-dose children
detail: UNICEF's latest immunisation estimate says India accounted for about 696,000 children who received no DTP vaccine dose in 2025. This is a remaining access gap even alongside high national DTP3 coverage, and the two measures should be read together.

title: Under-five mortality
detail: UNICEF's India country profile reports that the under-five mortality rate declined from 46.4 deaths per 1,000 live births in 2014 to 26.6 in 2024. This is a substantial population-health improvement, but it is not a single-policy attribution and national averages can conceal differences between states, districts, and income groups.

title: School completion
detail: UNICEF's India SDG dashboard reports 2021 completion rates of 94% for primary education, 86% for lower secondary, and 51% for upper secondary. The figures show strong primary completion but a substantial upper-secondary drop-off; they do not measure learning quality, attendance, or the reasons students leave school.
direction: High primary completion, major upper-secondary gap

title: Child malnutrition
detail: UNICEF's India SDG dashboard reports stunting among children under five at 32% in 2022 and wasting at 19% in 2020. These are serious child-health burdens; the years differ because the latest available data for each measure differ, and the figures are not a direct measure of one administration's causal impact.

title: Births attended by skilled health personnel
detail: UNICEF's India SDG dashboard records 81% of births attended by skilled health personnel in 2016. This is an older available SDG observation, not a current 2026 estimate; it is included to keep the evidence visible without pretending that the dashboard supplies a newer comparable value.

title: Basic drinking-water access
detail: UNICEF's India SDG dashboard reports that 93% of the population used basic drinking-water services in 2022. This measures access to an improved source with a short collection time; it does not establish that water is continuously available, affordable, safely managed, or free from contamination.
direction: High basic access, quality still separate

title: Basic handwashing facilities
detail: UNICEF's India SDG dashboard reports that 76% of the population had a basic handwashing facility with soap and water available on premises in 2022. This is a household-service measure and does not establish that people can use the facility consistently or that institutions such as schools and health facilities have equivalent coverage.

title: Total fertility rate
detail: UNICEF's statistical compendia report India's total fertility rate at 2.3 live births per woman in 2013 and 2.0 in 2023. This is a demographic transition measure, not a government performance score; it also does not describe fertility differences by state, income, education, or access to reproductive healthcare.

title: Women's labour-force participation
detail: MoSPI's Periodic Labour Force Survey reports the usual-status labour-force participation rate for women aged 15 years and above rising from 23.3% in 2017-18 to 41.7% in 2023-24. This is a major participation increase, but LFPR includes people working or actively seeking work; it does not measure pay, hours, job security, occupational segregation, unpaid care work, or whether work is formal.

title: Road-crash deaths
detail: The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways recorded 139,671 people killed in road crashes in 2014 and 172,890 in 2023. The 2023 report also recorded 480,583 crashes and 462,825 injuries. These are police-reported road-safety statistics; they are not the same as all transport deaths, and the series does not by itself identify which policies or behaviours caused the change.

title: Road-crash severity
detail: Using the Ministry's reported totals, deaths per 100 reported crashes rose from about 28.5 in 2014 (139,671 deaths across 489,400 crashes) to 36.0 in 2023 (172,890 deaths across 480,583 crashes). This derived severity ratio is not a fatality risk for an individual journey: reporting practices, crash composition, medical response, vehicle mix, and exposure all matter.

title: Women's worker-population ratio
detail: The official PLFS usual-status worker-population ratio for women aged 15 years and above rose from 22.0% in 2017-18 to 40.3% in 2023-24. WPR measures the share employed, unlike LFPR which also includes people actively seeking work. It still does not establish pay, hours, job security, formality, occupational quality, or the scale of unpaid work.

title: Anaemia among women aged 15-49
detail: Government health statistics report anaemia prevalence among all women aged 15-49 rising from 53.1% in NFHS-4 (2015-16) to 57.0% in NFHS-5 (2019-21). This is a serious health burden despite the Anaemia Mukt Bharat programme. The Ministry cautions that NFHS-5 used capillary blood testing, so results should not be treated as a perfectly like-for-like laboratory series with every earlier survey.

title: Anaemia among children aged 6-59 months
detail: Government and parliamentary health data report anaemia among children aged 6-59 months rising from 58.6% in NFHS-4 (2015-16) to 67.1% in NFHS-5 (2019-21). This is a severe child-health burden and should be read alongside the site's stunting and wasting measures. NFHS-5 used capillary blood testing, so the survey rounds are not a perfectly like-for-like laboratory series.

title: Anaemia among adolescent girls aged 15-19
detail: NFHS comparison data report anaemia among adolescent girls aged 15-19 rising from 54.1% in NFHS-4 (2015-16) to 59.1% in NFHS-5 (2019-21). The measure is distinct from anaemia among children and all women aged 15-49. NFHS-5 used capillary blood testing, so the survey rounds require methodological caution when compared directly.

title: Anaemia among adolescent boys aged 15-19
detail: NFHS comparison data report anaemia among adolescent boys aged 15-19 rising from 29.2% in NFHS-4 (2015-16) to 31.1% in NFHS-5 (2019-21). The threshold used for boys is haemoglobin below 13.0 g/dL, so this should not be directly compared with the girls' threshold. NFHS-5 used capillary blood testing, adding another comparability caveat.

title: Unemployment rate, usual status
detail: The official PLFS usual-status (ps+ss) unemployment rate for people aged 15 years and above fell from 6.0% in 2017-18 to 3.2% in 2023-24. This is a survey estimate under the usual-status definition. It should not be read as a measure of job quality, earnings, underemployment, informal work, or discouraged workers outside the labour force.

title: Reported suicides
detail: The National Health Profile reproduces NCRB totals showing reported suicides rising from 131,666 in 2014 to 164,033 in 2021. These are registered or reported deaths in the NCRB series, not a complete measure of all suicides; under-reporting, classification, registration quality, and population growth affect interpretation. The series also includes a separate transgender category from 2014 onward.

title: Multidimensional poverty headcount
detail: NITI Aayog's National Multidimensional Poverty Index reports the national headcount ratio falling from 24.85% in 2015-16 to 14.96% in 2019-21, a 9.89 percentage-point decline. The index combines deprivations across health, education, and living standards; it is not the same as income poverty. NITI Aayog later published a separate projected estimate for 2022-23, which is not substituted into this survey comparison.

title: Youth unemployment rate
detail: Government figures based on PLFS usual-status estimates report the unemployment rate for youth aged 15-29 falling from 17.8% in 2017-18 to 10.2% in 2023-24. This is a survey estimate under the usual-status definition; it does not measure job quality, wages, underemployment, or the share of young people outside the labour force and not seeking work. The 2023-24 period covers July 2023 to June 2024.

title: Average nominal salary of regular wage employees
value: Rs 16,848 in Apr-Jun 2018 to Rs 20,039 in Apr-Jun 2023
detail: A PIB release citing PLFS data reports average monthly wage or salary earnings for regular wage or salaried employees rising from Rs 16,848 in April-June 2018 to Rs 20,039 in April-June 2023, an 18.94% nominal increase. This is not inflation-adjusted, so it cannot by itself show whether purchasing power or real wages improved. It also excludes other worker categories and does not measure job security or benefits.

title: Estimated total employment
detail: A Ministry of Labour and Employment release cites RBI KLEMS estimates of total employment rising from 47.15 crore in 2014-15 to 64.33 crore in 2023-24. This is an estimated employment stock, not a count of jobs created during the period. KLEMS is a separate statistical framework from PLFS, so the figures should not be combined mechanically with survey unemployment or treated as a direct causal measure of government policy.

title: Women-owned proprietary establishments
detail: A government release citing the Annual Survey of Unincorporated Sector Enterprises reports the share of women-owned proprietary establishments rising from 17.4% in 2010-11 to 26.2% in 2023-24. This measures ownership of surveyed proprietary establishments, not women's total employment, business revenue, business survival, access to credit, or control over assets.

title: Internet freedom score
detail: Freedom House's Freedom on the Net assessments scored India at 55 out of 100 in 2019 and 50 out of 100 in 2024, with both editions classifying India as Partly Free. The scale runs from 0 (least free) to 100 (most free). The report discusses shutdowns, content blocking, arrests for online activity, and restrictions on digital expression; this is an external assessment, not a count of all censorship incidents or a sole causal verdict on one government.

title: Urban-rural internet penetration gap
detail: Freedom House cites TRAI data reporting internet penetration of 111.8% in urban areas and 44.2% in rural areas in March 2024. Urban penetration above 100% reflects multiple subscriptions per person and is not the share of urban residents online. The comparison shows a substantial subscription-density gap, but it does not measure quality, affordability, device access, or meaningful use.

title: Gender internet-access gap
value: 33% of adult women versus 57.1% of men
detail: Freedom House cites National Family Health Survey 2019-21 data showing that 33% of adult women had used the internet, compared with 57.1% of men. The 24.1 percentage-point gap is a gender-access measure, not a measure of speed, safety, frequency, digital skills, or economic benefit; rural and state-level differences are also substantial.

title: Internet subscriber base
value: 954.4 million subscribers; about 65.8% penetration in August 2024
detail: Freedom House cites TRAI's August 2024 subscriber data: 954.4 million internet subscribers and approximately 65.8% population penetration. Subscriber counts can include multiple connections held by one person and therefore are not a headcount of unique internet users; they also do not measure affordability, reliability, quality, or meaningful access.

title: World Happiness Report rank
detail: India's position improved from 126th of 143 countries in the World Happiness Report 2024 to 118th of 147 in the 2025 edition. The report measures average self-reported life evaluations and is not a direct measure of government performance, clinical depression, or every dimension of wellbeing; changes in country coverage also affect rank comparisons.
